content:
Good evening and welcome back. This lesson provides another look at Model number 5, which is a day
trading model. This is a brief preview of what will be covered in more detail in 2020 when we revisit the
models. The initial lesson is followed by an amplified perspective using examples, with detailed trading
plans starting in January 2020 for Model 1.
Here are some bullet point perspectives on how to implement this now:
- Every model going forward, for new members, (2016-2017 group already received all 12 models).
- We look for the inception of a move.
- We want to know where it's going.

section_title: ADDRESSING DISJOINTED LEARNING AND THE SIMPLE RECIPE
content:
Many questions arise, as you've learned a great deal in the mentorship, but everything feels disjointed. You
can see things when I show examples or fragmented in your own study, but you don't know where to place
the pieces together. I want to provide a simple recipe without all the details, allowing you to flesh out each
individual aspect yourself, utilizing the content taught in the core mentorship material.
When considering pairs for trading, this week (ending June 28th, 2019) we focused solely on the Dollar
CAD.

section_title: BEARISH BIAS FOR DOLLAR CAD AND SUPPORTING EVIDENCE
content:
Through my analysis and commentary, we felt the Dollar CAD was going to go lower. However, simply
believing the market will go lower is not enough. We need supporting evidence to back the premise that the
market will move in our direction and, specifically, to what level. What is the draw on liquidity?
We need to identify:
- A point of origin.
- Our criteria.

section_title: FAIR VALUE GAPS AS POINT OF ORIGIN AND BIAS DETERMINATION
content:
We've spent a great deal of time on fair value gaps in recent weeks and months. Much like order blocks,
once you understand the central tenets, fair value gaps are fairly easy to spot in price and are not
ambiguous; they are very clear. Our point of origin, or the premise of the setups, will be fair value gaps,
which we will incorporate with Model number 5.
We must determine our bias by consulting higher time frame charts. On the weekly chart, we anticipate a
weekly range expansion. This is what makes our high probability setups truly high probability. Today's
market close, combined with a weekly range that was down, indicates the direction.

My forte as a trader, what makes me ICT, is working within this framework every single week. This single
candle is the only thing I'm honestly trying to forecast in my own analysis with the highest degree of
probability and accuracy. While you've heard me discuss longer-term market movements, I do not trade
that way.
My expertise is day trading and very short-term trading, specifically the weekly range. I believe it suits me
best.

section_title: FLEXIBILITY IN TRADING STYLES AND MASTERY EXPECTATIONS
content:
This approach may not be the best for you, which is why I've taught all components of price action trading,
allowing you to be anything from a position trader to a scalper if you choose. The highest order of a student
would be to use all these things when valid, but none of you are close to doing that yet.
It's realistic to acknowledge if you don't feel up to par, as it takes decades to reach that level, and you may
never reach it. It's not required or needed to be profitable.

section_title: DOLLAR CAD BEARISH BIAS AND POWER OF THREE APPLICATION
content:
We determined our bias is bearish. How did I arrive at this bias for Dollar CAD, focusing on it over Euro and
Cable this past week? The higher time frame weekly chart showed a down close candle, or an expansion
on the downside. We're not trying to predict the close, but rather looking for an expansion in one direction
over another. We are incorporating Power of Three on the weekly chart, looking for an open with very
modest upside (if any) and the majority of the range on the downside. So, our Power of Three is bearish for
the weekly range, indicating we want to be shorting.
Why are we expecting the price of Dollar CAD to go lower? We're looking for the draw on liquidity at this old
low. The market has been heavy; we filled in a fair value gap (a buy-side inefficiency) back here. We had
recently entered a bearish stance for near-term dollar gold prices. I mentioned watching the gold market
because if it went higher, it would make it easier for the dollar to go lower and for foreign currencies to have
a higher probability to rally. This pair (Dollar CAD) begins with the dollar, so a drop here means the dollar is
falling weaker against the Canadian dollar. If we're bearish on the dollar, this pair should have very little
opposition or resistance in terms of momentum reaching for downside objectives.

section_title: UTILIZING PD MATRIX AND DAILY RANGE EXPANSION
content:
When we use our PD Matrix, we're looking for discount arrays that the market would logically reach for.
There's nothing really in here to frame this down close candle, so we can't use it as a bearish down close
representing a bullish order block because we already consumed all that here. We can consider:
- The lowest down close, which would be the rejection block, which it handsomely traded to.
- The old low.
- The sell-side liquidity resting below that, which is exactly what I discussed in previous commentaries on
Dollar CAD.
So, we have our draw on liquidity and the weekly range expansion, leading us to believe the market will go
lower. We can then drop into a daily chart and anticipate each daily range to expand downward, reaching
into that old daily low (1.3068, specifically 1.30677, rounded to 1.3068 for most brokers).

section_title: STALKING HIGH PROBABILITY FAIR VALUE GAPS
content:
The next procedure is to stalk a fair value gap every day using the previous day's range. I recently taught
that high probability fair value gaps are going to be in the lower 50% of the previous daily range when the
market is bearish. Therefore, fair value gaps you would look to sell into (go short) would generally form in
the lower half of the previous day's range.
Conversely, if we were looking at a bullish market, the high probability fair value gaps would appear or form
(the gap would exist) in the previous day's range's upper 50%. This would make it a high probability fair
value gap to go long in, when all other higher time frame factors indicate higher prices. However, as it were,
we were talking about the Dollar CAD being bearish and looking for the old low to be attacked, so sell-side
liquidity was our objective or the terminus.

section_title: KILL ZONES AND MODEL 5 SPECIFIC ENTRY OPPORTUNITIES
content:
Our analysis led to a directional bias and a target price. Every individual day, we would stalk fair value gaps
to go short in, but they have to form during a Kill Zone. Specifically, the first return back to a fair value gap
during a Kill Zone: London Kill Zone or New York Kill Zone, which is specific for this price action model.
You are not limited to just trading London and New York. While the framework for this model is such, you
can also trade the Asian Kill Zone and, if inclined, the New York Close Kill Zone. However, New York Close
Kill Zones are typically predisposed to longer-term position entries, like continuations into larger moves,
similar to London Close.

section_title: INSTITUTIONAL ORDERFLOW ENTRY DRILL (IOFED) AND SETUP ABUNDANCE
content:
When we have that first return to fair value gaps during a Kill Zone, what are we using for our entry? I
recently taught you the Institutional Orderflow Entry Drill (IOFED), where you practiced and did four weeks
of backtesting, then four weeks of forward testing. You should have all that data now, which should have
illustrated a numerous amount of setups and a plethora of pips available more than you probably thought.
There is no shortage of setups or opportunities. You don't need to trade every pair. The homework
assignment with backtesting showed that once you know what you're looking for, you don't have to stick to
one or two pairs. While learning, you want to do that, but if you want a basket approach using a larger
number of assets to find and filter setups, your own homework has proven there is no shortage using the
fair value gap and the Institutional Orderflow Entry Drill technique for entry.
This is the low-end entry. You're not looking for the entire gap to fill, nor for consequent encroachment.
You're using the lowest point of entry for selling short in a bearish fair value gap, or the highest point of the
gap when it's a bullish fair value gap.

section_title: MODEL 5 TARGETS, GRADUATING TO LARGER MOVES, AND HOLISTIC
content:
UNDERSTANDING
Once we have the setup in mind and see it in the chart, we know where the price is most likely to expand
on the daily and weekly using Model 5. We can see the confluence of intraday standard deviations on F-L
- O-U-T (Fair Value Gaps, Liquidity, Order Blocks, Undervalued, Overvalued, Trending), on Central Bank
Dealers Range, and on the Asian Range. This gives us targets every single day.
This model specifically aims for 40 to 50 pips per setup. That's the premise or focus of this particular model.
You can graduate into larger 'one shot one kill' scenarios by leaving a portion of the trade on. Some might
ask why not just let it go for a one shot one kill if you believe it will go further than 40-50 pips; you can do
that. This could be a stepping stone to achieving these larger weekly range expansions that go beyond 50
pips.
Alternatively, it can be utilized as a modular approach to getting your 50 to 75 pips a week, like I do. I don't
always get the 75 pip high-end in one setup; sometimes it requires two trades, or even four if I've taken a
loss or missed a move, or exited a position as a scratch. I then have to re-enter another transaction to meet
my weekly goal. Don't think this model is limited to this. The approach behind these 12 models was to lay
foundations so that when you understand and incorporate them all, you will have a complete understanding
of price action and can work within any model and overlap with them. This provides an unsurpassed depth
of understanding in the trading community.

section_title: PRACTICAL APPLICATION AND HIGH PROBABILITY TRADING DAYS
content:
Let's go over to a 15-minute timeframe. The shaded area here simply represents 40 pips; this is our low
- end objective for this model. Nothing more to it than that. I'm going to show you the day dividers: Friday's
trading, Thursday, Wednesday, Tuesday, and Monday. If we were to take this range and place it over
significant highs, you can see there was simply no real resistance to seeing price get to a 40-pip run on
Thursday. Whether you use that high or this high, it easily offered 40 pips. I'll go into the details in a
moment, as some of you might see this as entirely hindsight.
- We had a 40-pip run here from this high, going a little further, certainly offering 50 pips on that day.
- Here, 40 pips was a no-brainer as well.
- We had a late-day New York setup that offered another 40 pips.
Thursday, Wednesday, and Tuesday were high probability days, really nice. Even though this model is not
limited to Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday trading, you can filter it by limiting it to these days. In my opinion,
Tuesday and Wednesday are the highest probability days, so I like to use those days for most of my
hunting. It doesn't mean I'll get it, or capture it, or that it will set up; it may require me to go into Thursday to
get my haul for the week.
